content_html:
MGT10009 Contemporary Management Principles
Lecture 12a Managing Technology & Innovation
Dr David Qian dqian@swin.edu.au
Peter Moran pmoran@swin.edu.au

Learning Objectives
17-1 	List the types of processes that spur development of new technologies.
17-2 	Describe how technologies proceed through a life cycle.
17-3 	Discuss ways to manage technology for competitive advantage.
17-4 	Summarize how to assess technology needs.
17-5 	Identify alternative methods of pursuing technological innovation.
17-6 	Define key roles in managing technology.
17-7 	Describe the characteristics of innovative organizations.
17-8 	Describe the characteristics of successful development projects.
‹#›

Management in Action Elon Musk: Controversial Leader Embraces Tech’s Possibilities and Challenges
Elon Musk’s passions are many and varied; many people believe that’s the reason he is a successful innovator and entrepreneur, launching multibillion-dollar ventures in fields as different as digital payment systems, solar panels, automobiles, and space travel. He is a co-founder of PayPal (later sold to eBay); founder and C E O of the commercial rocket company SpaceX; and C E O of Tesla, the maker of electric and self-driving cars. These and other industry-changing ventures have made him one of the wealthiest and most influential—and controversial—people in the world.
‹#›

Technology and Innovation
Technology:
The systematic application of scientific knowledge to a new product, process, or service.
Innovation:
Changes in method or technology.
Positive, useful departure from previous ways of doing things.
‹#›

Exhibit 17.1 Fundamental Types of Innovation
Product innovation:
Drones that fly themselves and can follow people through forests and around bends.
Foldable phones and rollup TVs.
Process innovation
Robotic and robotic-assisted surgery.
More natural and fluid voice-driven interactions with digital assistants.
Business model innovation:
Al-powered chatbots serving customers.
Blockchain technology cutting transaction costs and risk.
‹#›

Driving Forces of Technological Development
Need or demand.
Feasibility.
Practicality.
Resource availability.
Entrepreneurialism.
‹#›

Exhibit 17.2 The Technology Life Cycle
Access the text alternative for slide image
‹#›

SPOTLIGHT ON… Reducing Unconscious Biases with Tech
One set of challenges to creating diverse and inclusive workplaces are ways of behaving and thinking that we are unaware of. Such unconscious biases, can have profound impacts, from recruiting and hiring to team collaboration and productivity. Because everyone harbors unconscious biases, and because these biases are difficult to detect, companies have struggled to mitigate their influence. Increasingly, firms are turning to technology to help solve this problem.
How effective do you think the use of technology can really be in better understanding and developing inclusive workplaces, and what other uses can you envision?
What challenges do you see as technology is deployed to better understand and mitigate employee biases?
‹#›

Exhibit 17.3 Technology Dissemination Pattern and Adopter Categories
Access the text alternative for slide image
‹#›

Diffusion of Technological Innovations
An innovation will spread quickly if it:
Has a great advantage over its predecessor.
Is compatible with existing systems, procedures, infrastructures, and ways of thinking.
Has less rather than greater complexity.
Can be tried or tested easily without significant cost or commitment.
Can be observed and copied easily.
‹#›

Technology Leadership and Followership
Disruptive innovation:
A process by which a product, service, or business model takes root initially in simple applications at the bottom of a market and then moves “up market,” eventually displacing established competitors.
‹#›

Exhibit 17.4 Advantages and Disadvantages of Technology Leadership
Advantages
Disadvantages
First-mover advantage
Greater risks
Little or no competition
Cost of technology development
Greater efficiency
Costs of market development and customer education
Higher profit margins
Infrastructure costs
Sustainable advantage
Costs of learning and eliminating defects
Reputation for innovation
Possible cannibalization of existing products
Establishments of entry barriers
Occupation of best market niches
Opportunities to learn
‹#›

Technology Followership
The decision on when to adopt new technology also depends on the potential benefits of the new technology, as well as the organization’s technology skills
Following the technology leader can save development expense.
‹#›

Assessing Technology Needs
Technology audit:
Process of clarifying the key technologies on which an organization depends and identifies which technologies are most important.
Four categories to measure:
Emerging technologies.
Pacing technologies.
Key technologies.
Base technologies.
‹#›

Assessing External Technological Trends
Benchmarking:
The process of comparing the organization’s current practices and technologies with those of other companies.
Scanning:
Focuses on what is being developed.
Places greater emphasis on identifying and monitoring the sources of new technologies for an industry.
‹#›

Making Technology Decisions
Technology decision making must balance the many interrelated factors including:
Anticipated market receptiveness.
Technological feasibility.
Economic viability.
Anticipated capability development.
Organizational suitability.
‹#›

Exhibit 17.5 Key Considerations in Technology Decisions
Considerations
Sample Contexts
Market receptiveness—assess external demand for the technology (short/long run)
Digital voice assistants, wearable technology, water conserving washers
Technological feasibility—evaluate technical barriers to progress
Deep-sea oil exploration and safe autonomous driving vehicles
Economic viability—examine any cost considerations and forecast profitability
Solar energy and in-home robotic technology
Competence development—determine whether current capabilities are sufficient
Information technology in healthcare and digital technology in cameras
Organizational suitability—assess the fit with culture and managerial systems
Steel companies focusing on creativity and innovation
‹#›

Management in Action Racing to Produce the World’s Best Electric Vehicle
For Tesla to lead in what founder and C E O Elon Musk sees as the future of driving, it must produce and sell enough electric vehicles (E V s) at scale to earn a sufficient profit.
What are the advantages and disadvantages to Tesla Motors being a technology leader?
Check in with Tesla. How is Tesla Motors faring in comparison to its competitors? How well has it overcome its challenges?
‹#›

Sourcing and Acquiring New Technologies
Make-or-buy decision
The question an organization asks itself about whether to acquire new technology from an outside source or develop it itself.
Sources of technology:
Internal development.
Purchase.
Contracted development.
Licensing.
Technology trading.
Research partnerships and joint ventures.
Acquiring ownership.
‹#›

SPOTLIGHT ON… Science + Business = Limitless Possibilities?
Biochemist, entrepreneur, Noble Prize winner—these all describe Jennifer Doudna, a scientist who conducted pioneering research on what’s known as CRISPR technology, a genome editing method. Doudna is now launching a number of start-up companies to solve many of the world’s most vexing problems.
C R I S P R technology presents many opportunities to help humanity. What do you see as potential risks and ethical challenges that come with such precise ability to alter the human genome?
How does Doudna’s start-up efforts with C R I S P R technology align with the driving forces of technological development described in this chapter?
‹#›

Exhibit 17.6 Technology Acquisition Options
Access the text alternative for slide image
‹#›

Organizing for Innovation
Chief information officer (C I O):
Executive in charge of information technology strategy and development.
Coordinates the technological efforts of the various business units.
Identifies ways that technology can support the company’s strategy.
Supervises new-technology development.
Assesses technological implications of major strategic initiatives such as acquisitions, new ventures, and strategic alliances.
‹#›

Key Technology Roles
Technical innovator:
A person who develops a new technology or has the key skills to install and operate the technology.
Product champion:
A person who promotes a new technology throughout the organization in effort to obtain acceptance of and support for it.
Executive champion:
An executive who supports a new technology and protects the product champion.
‹#›

Innovation Culture
Innovative companies have many balls in the air at all times, with many people trying many new ideas. Most are strikeouts, but a few home runs turn a company into an innovative star.
This management perspective and approach fosters creative thinking and innovative efforts throughout the ranks.
‹#›

Design Thinking
A human-centered approach to problem solving and solution finding that is based on nonlinear iterations of inspiration, ideation, and implementation
Design teams consist of people with diverse expertise developing deep understanding of customer needs and desires through observation and experimentation.
‹#›

Organizational Efforts
Development project:
A focused organizational effort to create a new product or process via technological advances.
Sociotechnical systems:
An approach to job design that attempts to redesign tasks to optimize operation of a new technology while preserving employees’ interpersonal relationships and other human aspects of the work.
‹#›

Management in Action Will SpaceX Make Humans Multiplanetary?
Have you ever wanted to see Mars up close? Elon Musk, the entrepreneur behind SpaceX, hopes to land a piloted craft on Mars by 2026. His company reached an engineering milestone in 2017 by creating the first reusable spacecraft. But the ultimate goal, according to Musk, is to “establish Mars as a self-sustaining civilization.”
To your surprise, you are hired to replace Elon Musk. You decide to focus your efforts. Which of his enterprises would you sell, and which would you continue to pursue? Explain your choices, from a business standpoint and a personal-interest standpoint.
Check up on Musk's various enterprises. How are they doing now? What has gone right, what has gone wrong, and what are the biggest challenges today?
‹#›

In Review
List the types of processes that spur development of new technologies.
Describe how technologies proceed through a life cycle.
Discuss ways to manage technology for competitive advantage.
Summarize how to assess technology needs.
Identify alternative methods of pursuing technological innovation.
Define key roles in managing technology.
Describe the characteristics of innovative organizations.
Describe the characteristics of successful development projects.
‹#›
